title: Gate and share M-extension ALU hardware
motivation: The current ALU unconditionally forms three independent 64-bit products for every instruction, even though CoreMark is dominated by RV32I arithmetic, branches, and memory traffic. On the Tang Nano 20K this likely burns LUTs and routing and may sit on or near the EX-stage critical path despite M operations being rare in the benchmark.

title: Move RV32M to multicycle side unit
motivation: The current EX stage contains full combinational RV32M multiply and divide logic inside the same ALU case as single-cycle integer operations. On the Tang Nano 20K this likely contributes a long critical path and high LUT pressure even though CoreMark's hot loop is dominated by simple integer, branch, and memory operations. Removing the divider and wide multiplier from the regular EX datapath should improve Fmax and may also improve placement by reducing EX fan-in.

hypothesis.hypothesis: Restructure the pipeline so RV32M operations dispatch from EX into a dedicated multicycle M unit while ordinary RV32I ALU, branch, jump, and address-generation operations remain single-cycle. When an M instruction reaches EX, hold IF/ID/EX behind it until the unit returns the result, then capture that result into EX/MEM and retire normally through the existing MEM/WB and RVFI path. title: Add tiny backward-branch predictor
motivation:
The current five-stage core resolves every conditional branch in EX and then
flushes two younger wrong-path instructions on taken branches. CoreMark has
many small loop backedges, so the static not-taken policy wastes cycles on the
hottest control-flow pattern while also exposing random instruction stalls to
extra refetch work.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a minimal static predictor in fetch/decode that predicts backward
conditional branches taken and redirects the PC to the decoded B-immediate
target before EX. Carry predicted-next-PC metadata through ID/EX so EX can
distinguish correct predictions from mispredictions, only flushing on actual
mismatches. This should remove most taken-loop branch penalties with very low
LUT cost and little Fmax risk compared with a dynamic BHT/BTB.

title: Suppress false load-use stalls
motivation:
The current hazard unit stalls on any instruction following a load when its
rd matches bits [19:15] or [24:20] of the next instruction. Those bit fields
are not always live source operands: I-type ALU/load/JALR only reads rs1,
stores and branches read both, and U/J/FENCE/EBREAK read neither. False
load-use bubbles directly reduce CoreMark iterations/cycle and become more
expensive under the random instruction/data stall model.

title: Bypass ready load data to EX
motivation: The current five-stage pipeline pays a mandatory one-cycle load-use bubble whenever the instruction immediately after a load consumes the loaded register. CoreMark's list and matrix kernels contain many pointer and array dependent loads, and the benchmark bus model often has dmem ready in the same cycle. Recovering those bubbles should improve iterations/cycle without changing the retirement width or adding the broad pipeline stalls that hurt the prior structural M-unit attempt.

title: Correct ID backward-branch predictor
motivation:
The current five-stage core pays a two-instruction flush penalty on every taken conditional branch because branches resolve in EX and fetch is effectively static not-taken. CoreMark's inner loops should contain frequent backward taken branches, so recovering even part of that penalty can improve iterations per cycle without touching the already successful M-extension ALU optimization.

The previous predictor attempt identified the right low-cost direction but failed because EX compared against IF fall-through metadata rather than the branch's decoded target, so nominally correct backward predictions still looked wrong. A corrected metadata path is the most promising predictor-only follow-up.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a minimal static predictor that operates after decode: when ID sees a valid conditional branch with a negative B-immediate, redirect fetch to the decoded branch target and mark that branch with predicted_taken and predicted_pc_next in the ID/EX payload. EX then computes the resolved next PC for branches and jumps and redirects only when resolved_pc_next differs from the carried predicted_pc_next.

This keeps the predictor tiny: no BHT, no BTB, no tags, and no speculative history state. Forward progress and RVFI ordering remain anchored at the existing MEM/WB retirement point, while correct backward loop predictions avoid fetching and later flushing the two fall-through instructions behind each taken loop branch.

title: Share RV32M divide datapath
motivation:
The current best result came from a narrow ALU micro-optimization: sharing and gating the MUL-family datapath improved Tang Nano Fmax without adding pipeline stalls. The remaining RV32M divide/remainder logic still exposes separate signed and unsigned / and % operators in the combinational ALU case, which can leave yosys with multiple wide divider/remainder cones and extra muxing even though CoreMark mostly executes simpler RV32I operations.

Prior attempts to move M-extension work into a multicycle side unit regressed because the added pipeline holds cost more than the Fmax gain. A same-cycle datapath sharing pass keeps the existing timing model and RVFI retirement behavior while attacking the remaining large arithmetic hardware in the common EX block.

title: Register fetch-decode boundary
motivation: The current core has no real IF/ID pipeline register: the fetched instruction and PC flow combinationally from the PC/imem side into decoder, immediate generation, regfile read addressing, hazard detection, and the ID/EX register. That likely creates a broad timing path through external instruction data and decode fanout, and prior predictor work showed that adding more fetch/decode combinational logic can severely hurt Fmax.

This should shorten the highest-fanout front-end timing path and improve FPGA Fmax with only a small control change and no new load-data or RV32M critical path.

title: Add tiny fetch BTB predictor
motivation:
The current core still resolves all branches and jumps in EX, so every taken
control transfer pays a two-younger-instruction flush. Prior predictor
attempts showed that decode-time backward prediction is risky on this FPGA
because the imem_data-to-decode-to-target-add path can become part of the
fetch critical path. A fetch-PC-indexed BTB avoids that failed path while
still targeting CoreMark's hot loop backedges and repeated call/return-like
JAL/JALR targets.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a very small direct-mapped BTB in the fetch stage, indexed only by the
registered PC and updated from EX when a branch or jump resolves. Each entry
stores valid, tag, target, and a one-bit or two-bit taken confidence bit;
fetch predicts to the stored target only on a tag hit, otherwise it keeps the
existing pc+4 behavior. Carry predicted_pc_next through IF/ID and ID/EX so EX
redirects only when the resolved next PC differs from the prediction, and
update or allocate entries for taken branches/JAL/JALR while weakening or
clearing entries on not-taken branches.

This is the most promising predictor-only follow-up because it removes the
known ID predictor critical path, learns actual targets instead of relying on
a static backward heuristic, and should recover taken-control IPC on stable
CoreMark loops with modest LUT/FF cost.

title: Remove regfile reset fanout
motivation:
The current register file synchronously resets all 32 architectural registers, which forces reset muxing and routing across 1024 state bits. On a small FPGA this can prevent efficient distributed-RAM inference and add reset fanout to a block that is read on the decode critical path.

RV32I only requires x0 to read as zero; the other architectural registers do not need a defined hardware reset value as long as software initializes the registers it uses and RVFI reports the actual values read. Removing this reset is a narrow micro-optimization aimed at improving Fmax and possibly LUT pressure without changing pipeline depth, stalls, forwarding, memory behavior, or retirement width.

RV32I only requires x0 to read as zero; the other architectural registers do not need a defined hardware reset value as long as software initializes the registers it uses and RVFI reports the actual values read. Removing this reset is a narrow micro-optimization aimed at improving Fmax and possibly LUT pressure without changing pipeline depth, stalls, forwarding, memory behavior, or retirement width.

Refactor rtl/reg_file.sv so reset no longer clears regs[1:31]. Keep x0 hardwired through the existing read and write gating, preserve write-first same-cycle bypass behavior, and leave the write port synchronous.

This should let yosys/nextpnr implement the 32x32 two-read/one-write file with less reset-related muxing and routing, potentially improving the decode/regfile-read timing path and reducing LUT/FF pressure. Because the change only affects power-on contents of nonzero registers, CoreMark and formal should remain correct if all live software state is initialized architecturally through writes before use.

title: Add RAM store buffer
motivation:
The current pipeline freezes all younger work whenever a store in EX/MEM sees
io_dmemReady low, even though stores do not produce a register result for the
following instructions. Under the benchmark's random dmem backpressure model,
CoreMark can lose cycles waiting on regular RAM stores that could instead be
queued and drained in order while independent ALU/control work continues.

Prior structural attempts that changed the M datapath, load-use timing, or the
fetch/decode boundary regressed because they lengthened hot combinational paths
or inserted broad stalls. A one-entry store buffer is a narrower structural
change: it targets store-only dmem stalls, keeps loads conservative, and avoids
new ALU or branch critical-path logic.

title: Predict JAL in decode
motivation:
The current core resolves every taken control transfer in EX, so unconditional
JAL instructions pay the same two-younger-instruction flush penalty as taken
conditional branches. Prior predictor attempts showed that conditional
backward prediction lengthened the imem/decode/target path and that mutable
BTB arrays complicated formal convergence, so the next predictor experiment
should target a smaller, deterministic subset of control flow.

title: Gate divider operands only
motivation:

target speculation, and no change to branch condition resolution. It should
recover the hottest loop backedge penalties after warm-up while limiting Fmax

title: Trim dead pipeline metadata
motivation: The current best core has already benefited from narrow datapath cleanup, while later attempts to add gating, prediction, buffering, or divider rewrites mostly lost Fmax or broke formal. There are still dead bits carried through hot pipeline bundles: ctrl.is_lui is decoded but never consumed, and EX/MEM branch_taken/branch_target are written but not read by MEM, WB, RVFI, hazard, or forwarding logic. On a small FPGA these unused flops and their assignment cones can still consume routing, reset/load muxing, and placement freedom.

title: Bypass ALU for memory addresses
motivation:
The current EX stage routes load and store effective-address generation through
the same combinational ALU result mux used for arithmetic, branches, jumps,
and RV32M operations. CoreMark executes many memory operations, so their MEM
address path can inherit unnecessary muxing and arithmetic fanout even though
the required operation is only rs1 + immediate.

Prior structural attempts that added front-end state, store queues, or load
bypasses regressed because they added control complexity or new critical
paths. A narrower structural split inside EX keeps the pipeline timing and IPC
identical while trying to shorten a hot address-generation path.

stall behavior, or RVFI metadata. Because the change is a local retiming/split
and not a predictor or buffer, it has lower correctness and formal risk than
the failed structural experiments.

title: Cache repeated JAL targets
motivation:
The core still pays a two-younger-instruction flush for every taken control
transfer. Prior predictor attempts show that decode-time target generation,
conditional branch prediction, and general mutable BTBs tend to lose FPGA
timing or formal convergence. A narrower predictor should target only a
deterministic, always-taken control-flow class while keeping instruction
decode and branch comparison out of the fetch PC critical path.

BTB by avoiding arrays, counters, conditional branches, and JALR targets.

small amount of registered state and a single PC compare/mux.

title: Mux load byte lanes
motivation:
The current MEM stage extracts byte and halfword loads with a variable right
shift by addr[1:0] * 8. CoreMark has frequent loads, and on the Tang Nano 20K
that small barrel-shifter style cone may sit on the dmem_rdata-to-MEM/WB path
and consume more LUT/routing than a direct byte-lane mux.

Recent micro-opt attempts that added operand gating, divider rewrites, or
pipeline metadata churn regressed, so the most promising next angle is a
behavior-preserving local simplification of an existing hot datapath rather
than adding new control or state.

title: Qualify load-use interlock sources
motivation:
The current load-use interlock compares a load destination against both raw rs1 and rs2 fields of the following instruction, even when the following opcode does not actually read one of those fields. That can inject unnecessary bubbles after loads in CoreMark's pointer-heavy code, reducing iterations/cycle without improving correctness.

A prior attempt at source qualification broke because it used opcode-level approximations and changed ID/EX operand metadata. The safer structural version is to add exact decoder-produced source-use bits and use them only in the hazard decision, leaving RVFI rs addresses/data and the existing forwarding metadata unchanged.

Extend the decoder/control path with uses_rs1 and uses_rs2 bits that are cleared only for instruction forms that architecturally do not read the corresponding register field. Feed those bits for the current IF/ID instruction into hazard_unit, and gate the load-use comparisons so a load only stalls when the younger instruction truly consumes the loaded rd.

This should recover occasional false load-use bubbles for OP-IMM, LOAD, LUI, AUIPC, JAL, FENCE, and EBREAK-like instructions while preserving the existing five-stage topology, RVFI metadata, single-retire nret=1 contract, and conservative real load-use stalls. The change adds no data bypass path, no predictor state, and no dmem/imem queueing, so the main risk is modest front-end control timing from the extra source-use decode.

title: Predict returns with tiny RAS
motivation: The existing core still resolves every JALR in EX, so function returns pay the normal taken-control flush penalty. Prior predictor attempts lost fitness when they added decode-time immediate target generation, fetch-side BTB/tag arrays, or broad predicted-PC metadata for many control-flow classes. CoreMark still has repeated call/return structure, so a narrower return-only predictor may recover useful IPC with less timing cost than general branch or JAL target prediction.

hypothesis.hypothesis:

When EX retires or resolves a legal JAL that writes x1 or x5, push the already-available pc_plus4 link address into a 2-entry RAS; when decode sees a valid JALR with rs1=x1 or x5, rd=x0, and imm=0, redirect fetch to the registered RAS top one cycle before EX would otherwise resolve it. Carry predicted_pc_next and predicted_valid for that instruction through ID/EX, and in EX suppress the normal JALR redirect only if the computed target exactly matches the carried prediction; otherwise redirect to the resolved target and repair or invalidate the RAS entry.

The critical added decode path is only opcode/rd/rs1/imm-zero qualification plus a mux selecting a registered RAS top, so the Fmax risk should be lower while still targeting frequent CoreMark returns.

title: Trim pipeline reset muxes
motivation:
The best successful changes for this core have been narrow timing and routing cleanups that preserve the five-stage behavior, while recent predictor, buffer, divider, and metadata edits mostly regressed. The current pipeline still resets entire packed ID/EX, EX/MEM, and MEM/WB records, adding reset muxing and fanout to many datapath bits whose values are architecturally ignored whenever valid is low.

Unlike removing reset from the architectural register file, trimming reset on invalid pipeline payload fields should not expose software-visible state. It targets placement and Fmax pressure in stage registers without changing IPC, branch policy, memory timing, RVFI order, or the nret=1 interface.

The expected benefit is modest but potentially positive Fmax on Tang Nano 20K, with lower risk than adding new state machines or datapath muxes because the architectural behavior of valid instructions remains unchanged.

title: Register writeback result
motivation:
The current MEM/WB stage carries both alu_result and read_data, then the WB
stage selects the final writeback value combinationally every cycle. That
selected value feeds the regfile write port, MEM/WB forwarding into EX, and
RVFI rd_wdata, so the small load-vs-ALU mux sits on several timing-relevant
fanout paths even though its decision is already known when MEM/WB captures.

Prior structural attempts that added front-end state, prediction metadata,
store buffering, or same-cycle load bypasses regressed by lengthening hotter
paths or changing stall behavior. This is a narrower pipeline retiming that
should preserve IPC exactly while shortening writeback and forwarding logic.

title: One-bit loop prediction
motivation: The current core still pays the full EX redirect flush on every taken conditional branch, but prior predictor attempts show that decode redirects, BTB arrays, JAL/JALR target caches, and 32-bit predicted-PC metadata all cost more FPGA fitness than they recover. CoreMark still has hot loop backedges, so the remaining predictor opportunity is to make the predictor narrower rather than more capable.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a single-entry taken-branch target predictor for resolved backward conditional branches only. Fetch compares the registered PC against one learned branch PC tag and, on a hit, uses the stored target as the next PC while carrying only a one-bit predicted_taken flag with that instruction. EX suppresses the normal taken-branch redirect when the branch was predicted taken and actually resolves taken; on a predicted-taken branch that resolves not-taken, EX redirects to the already-registered fall-through pc_plus4 and clears or weakens the entry.

This intentionally avoids the failed ingredients from earlier predictor attempts: no decode-time immediate-to-PC path, no predictor array, no JAL/JALR/return prediction, and no 32-bit predicted_pc_next comparison through the pipeline. The cost is one PC tag compare, one target mux, one learned target register, and a single metadata bit, which may recover repeated loop-backedge flushes with less Fmax damage than previous predictor designs.

title: Qualify EX/MEM forwarding
motivation: The forwarding unit currently gives EX/MEM priority for any instruction with reg_write set, including loads. A load's architectural writeback value is not available in EX/MEM; the existing load-use interlock ensures consumers only execute once the load has reached MEM/WB. Keeping loads in the EX/MEM forwarding match therefore leaves dead compare/select logic on the EX operand path and can momentarily select an unusable load address during held bubbles.

title: Drop dead AUIPC ALU mux
motivation: The current best bench core already precomputes PC+imm in ID and uses that registered value for AUIPC writeback in EX. However, the EX ALU input path still contains an is_auipc-controlled mux that forces alu_a to zero even though the ALU result is ignored for AUIPC. That mux adds control fanout and operand routing to the common ALU path used by arithmetic, load/store address generation, and branch support.

title: Clean divider EX handshake
motivation:
The current best core still carries full combinational DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU
operators in the ALU result cone, while the successful multiply sharing pass
already removed much of the always-live M-extension pressure. Prior broad
multicycle M-unit attempts regressed or broke because they stalled common
multiply operations and used ad hoc control that desynchronized EX/MEM and
RVFI payloads. A divider-only structural split remains attractive if the EX
stage owns a precise valid/hold/done handshake and captures the entire
instruction payload atomically with the divider result.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace only the real DIV/REM combinational datapath with an iterative or
staged divider unit that starts when a valid divider-class instruction enters
EX. While the divider is busy, hold PC, ID/EX, and the in-flight EX payload;
do not bubble EX/MEM with partial metadata. When done, commit exactly one
EX/MEM register update containing the original instruction metadata,
forwarded rs values, pc_next, control bits, and the final quotient or
remainder. Keep RV32I and the existing shared multiply family single-cycle.

This should improve Fmax by removing the remaining same-cycle divide and
remainder cones from the hot ALU path, with limited CoreMark IPC cost because
divider-class instructions are much rarer than ordinary ALU, branch, memory,
and multiply operations. The key structural change versus the broken prior
divider attempt is a single owner for divider state inside EX and an atomic
EX/MEM capture on completion, preserving RVFI order and payload alignment.

title: Cache redirect target instruction
motivation: Previous predictor attempts for this core mostly regressed because they put tag compares, target muxes, decode immediates, or predicted-PC metadata on the fetch PC path, and the latest loop predictor broke when prediction state was consulted at the wrong time. The remaining predictor opportunity is to avoid early PC speculation entirely while still reducing the penalty of repeated taken CoreMark loop backedges.

Add a one-entry redirect target instruction cache trained only by resolved taken backward conditional branches. When such a branch redirects to a target and the target instruction is subsequently fetched with imemReady, remember the branch PC, target PC, and first target instruction. title: Register store lane outputs
motivation:
The successful bench-core micro-optimizations have been narrow timing cleanups that keep the five-stage pipeline and RVFI contract unchanged. The MEM stage still rebuilds store byte replication and byte masks combinationally from EX/MEM every cycle, and those signals directly drive dmem outputs plus RVFI store fields on a path that also carries load extraction and misalignment gating.

Prior MEM lane extraction and forwarding changes regressed when they altered load-data muxing or added new bypass control. A store-only precompute avoids the load result path and should reduce output-side MEM mux depth for stores without changing stalls, branch policy, arithmetic, or retirement behavior.

title: Hazard-local source-use bits
motivation:
The current load-use interlock compares a load destination against both raw source fields of the following instruction, even when the following instruction does not architecturally consume one of those fields. CoreMark has enough pointer and immediate-heavy code that false load-use bubbles can reduce iterations per cycle, especially under the random stall model.

Two earlier source-qualification attempts broke because they either used approximate opcode logic or let source-use information alter RVFI/register metadata. The lesson is useful: source-use qualification should exist only in the hazard decision, while rs addresses, rs data, forwarding payloads, and retirement metadata remain exactly as they are today.

This is a structural control-path cleanup rather than a predictor or datapath rewrite. It preserves the five-stage pipeline, nret=1 RVFI contract, memory behavior, forwarding paths, and all retirement metadata, while removing unnecessary bubbles after loads followed by OP-IMM, LOAD, LUI, AUIPC, JAL, FENCE, EBREAK, or other one/no-source instructions. The expected upside is IPC rather than Fmax, with lower timing risk than store buffers, fetch buffers, same-cycle load bypassing, or branch prediction.

title: Delayed loop predictor
motivation:
The current core still loses two younger instructions on every taken loop
branch, but prior predictor attempts show that same-cycle fetch PC tag
compares, decode-immediate redirects, BTB/RAS state, and redirect-cycle
instruction injection usually cost more FPGA timing than they recover.
A predictor experiment is still worthwhile only if it avoids adding logic to
the normal imem_data-to-PC or PC-tag-hit-to-PC critical path.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a one-entry delayed loop predictor trained only by resolved taken
backward conditional branches. During fetch, compare the current registered
PC against the learned branch PC and register the hit/target metadata, but
do not use that same-cycle compare to choose the next PC. One cycle later,
while the branch is in decode, use the registered hit to redirect fetch to
the stored target and carry the exact predicted target with that branch into
EX. EX suppresses the normal taken-branch redirect only when the carried
prediction matches the resolved target; otherwise it falls back to the
existing redirect and flush behavior.

cycles, but it removes the predictor tag compare from the fetch PC update
cone that hurt previous one-entry loop predictors. It also avoids decode
immediate generation for prediction, arrays, counters, JAL/JALR/RAS cases,
and redirect-target instruction caching. The expected upside is modest, but
this is the narrowest remaining predictor-only path that can improve loop
IPC without recreating the known Tang Nano timing regressions.

title: Fold forwarding into EX
motivation:
The current core computes EX operand bypass selects in a standalone forward_unit, then routes two 2-bit selects plus both destination-register/control bundles across the top level into ex_stage. Prior micro-opts that changed ALU, MEM, branch prediction, or load-use behavior mostly regressed, so the next promising angle is a placement/routing cleanup that preserves the exact five-stage behavior and does not add any datapath cones.

Forwarding is on every ALU, branch, and load/store address cycle, and its compare outputs directly control the EX operand muxes. Keeping the compare and mux logic in one module may let yosys flatten and place the equality checks with the consuming muxes, while also removing top-level select wires from the critical EX operand path.

title: Retire PC-next in MEM
motivation: The only structural improvement that has held up so far was a pure retiming of PC-relative sums from EX back into ID. Most later structural attempts regressed by adding front-end PC muxes, same-cycle load-data paths, store-buffer control, or wider prediction metadata. The current EX stage still builds the RVFI next-PC value in the same block that resolves redirects, selects jump link data, runs forwarding, and captures EX/MEM, even though the RVFI pc_wdata value is not observed until retirement.

hypothesis.motivation: The only structural improvement that has held up so far was a pure retiming of PC-relative sums from EX back into ID. Most later structural attempts regressed by adding front-end PC muxes, same-cycle load-data paths, store-buffer control, or wider prediction metadata. The current EX stage still builds the RVFI next-PC value in the same block that resolves redirects, selects jump link data, runs forwarding, and captures EX/MEM, even though the RVFI pc_wdata value is not observed until retirement.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Move RVFI pc_next construction from EX into the MEM/WB capture path. EX should continue resolving branch/jump redirects exactly as today, but EX/MEM only needs to carry the resolved control-flow target and branch-taken state; mem_stage can compute the retiring pc_next from registered EX/MEM metadata as either pc+4 or the resolved target before writing MEM/WB.

This keeps the five-stage pipeline, nret=1 RVFI contract, load-use behavior, redirect timing, and CoreMark IPC unchanged. The expected benefit is Fmax from shortening the EX-stage register input cone and reducing fanout around the pc_next mux, while avoiding any new fetch-side feedback or predictor state that prior structural attempts showed to be expensive on Tang Nano 20K.

title: Use stalls for loop prediction
motivation: Prior predictor attempts on this core consistently lost fitness when they added tag compares, target muxes, decode immediates, or predicted-PC metadata to the normal zero-wait fetch path. CoreMark still spends cycles on taken loop backedges, but the random imem stall model creates cycles where fetch is already unable to deliver the sequential instruction. A predictor that only acts during those otherwise-lost stall windows may recover some loop-target latency without slowing the common fetch path.

hypothesis.motivation: Prior predictor attempts on this core consistently lost fitness when they added tag compares, target muxes, decode immediates, or predicted-PC metadata to the normal zero-wait fetch path. CoreMark still spends cycles on taken loop backedges, but the random imem stall model creates cycles where fetch is already unable to deliver the sequential instruction. A predictor that only acts during those otherwise-lost stall windows may recover some loop-target latency without slowing the common fetch path.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a one-entry EX-trained loop predictor for resolved taken backward conditional branches, but do not let it affect the normal `next_pc = pc + 4` fetch update when `io_imemReady` is high. When the current fetch PC matches the learned branch PC while `io_imemReady` is low, pre-arm a registered predicted target for the next cycle; if the pipeline is still waiting on instruction memory or is just resuming from the stall, use that registered target to redirect fetch and mark the branch with carried predicted metadata so EX can suppress only the matching taken redirect.

This intentionally recovers less than a full same-cycle predictor, but it keeps the PC tag compare and target selection off the common zero-wait fetch path that hurt previous predictors. The expected win is from hiding part of hot loop backedge recovery under random instruction stalls, with low LUT/state cost and no BTB arrays, RAS logic, decode-time immediates, or jump prediction.
